rulings.Case Title: CHRISTINE E LOPEZ VS. ESPECIALLY CATS VETERINARY HOSPITAL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, September 18, 2018, Line 3, DEFENDANTs ESPECIALLY CATS VETERINARY HOSPITAL, MANPRIT BUTTAR, GRACE CARTER and BUTTAR, INC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication The Court denies defendants Especially Cats Veterinary Hospital; Manprit S. Buttar, DVM; Grace I. Carter, DVM; and Buttar, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment or, alternatively summary adjudication. There are triable issues of material fact regarding the following issues: (1) Whether any act or omission on the part of defendants was a substantial factor in causing Shadow's death; (2) Whether Shadow's delayed diagnosis prevented Shadow from receiving treatment that would have extended Shadow's life; (3) Whether defendants' alleged breach of the standard of care caused loss of use to Ms. Lopez, by preventing the performance of life-extending surgery on her cat. With regard to the fraud claim, summary adjudication is denied because (1) defendants failed to shift the burden to plaintiff as to the alleged falsity of the statements and defendants' intent to deceive / knowledge of falsehood because they failed to include any material facts in the separate statement pertaining to these elements; (2) defendants did not address the pleadings for restitution of the money plaintiff spent in reliance on defendants' alleged misrepresentations. Finally, when there are no undisputed facts in a separate statement to support the motion, it must be denied as a matter of law. (United Community Church v. Carcin (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 327, 337.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff Christine Lopez must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/acm

rulings.Case Title: DON TUCKER ET AL VS. THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company Of Connecticuts Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs To Produce For In Camera Review Documents Withheld On Grounds Of Mediation Privilege
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 18, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANTS THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT, DIANE FRAZIER'S Motion To Compel Plaintiffs To Produce For In Camera Review Documents Withheld On Grounds Of Mediation Privilege. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion denied as untimely under CCP § 2031.310. Sanctions awarded against The Travelers Indemnity Company of Connecticut in the amount of $7000, to be paid to the Pillsbury Coleman firm within 10 days of this order. rulings.Case Title: ANH NGUYEN VS. PAUL J. BURNS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Maureen Fong Hinners; Declaration Of Jeremy J. Schroeder
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, September 18, 2018, Line 6, PART 1 OF 2 TENTATIVE RULINGS: DEFENDANT UNION PACIFIC CORPORATION's Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction; The Court grants defendant Union Pacific Corporation (UPC)'s motion to quash service of summons for lack of personal jurisdiction. Plaintiff's counsel agreed to an open extension for UPC to file its motion to quash. Plaintiff did not seek entry of default judgment after time to respond expired. Under Goddard v. Pollock (1974) 37 Cal.App.3d 137, 141, if a motion to quash is "belatedly filed, but at a time when a default has not yet been taken, the plaintiff has, in effect, granted the defendant additional time within which to plead, and he is not strictly in default." The motion is timely. Plaintiff has not shown evidence warranting personal jurisdiction over UPC in California. UPC lacks the requisite "continuous and systematic" contacts necessary for jurisdiction. (See International Shoe v. Washington (1945) 326 U.S. 310, 317). Defendant Union Pacific Railroad Company (UPRC) is UPC's principal operating company. UPC is a holding company with no direct engagement in UPRC's day-to-day operations in California. UPC is incorporated in Utah and has not purposefully availed itself of California's jurisdiction. In California "neither ownership nor control of a subsidiary corporation by a foreign parent corporation, without more, subjects the parent to the jurisdiction of the state where the subsidiary does business." (Sonora Diamond Corp. v. Superior Court (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 523, 540.) While due process and jurisdictional considerations should be applied "flexibly" to ensure that commercial defendants do not become "judgment proof" from actions taken in other states (see West Corp. v. Superior Court (Sanford) (2004) 116 Cal.App.4th 1167, 1180 (internal quotations omitted)), UPC has assured Plaintiff that UPRC has sufficient assets to satisfy any judgment rendered against it.

rulings.Case Title: ANH NGUYEN VS. PAUL J. BURNS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, September 18, 2018, LINE 6, PART 2 OF 2 TENTATIVE RULINGS, Motion To Quash Service Of Summons For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction An "alter ego" theory is inapplicable here. In California, in order to invoke the alter ego doctrine, there must be (1) a unity of interest and ownership between the corporation and its equitable owner that the separate entities do not in reality exist; and (2) an inequitable result if the acts in question are treated as those of the corporation alone. (Sonora Diamond, 83 Cal.App.4th at 538.) Plaintiff failed to present facts showing that UPC and UPRC are so intertwined as to be one entity. UPRC operates the trains, and UPC is a parent company with general executive control that does not own or operate assets in California. Alter ego theory is "an imprecise substitute for the appropriate jurisdictional question. The proper jurisdictional question is ¿ whether the defendant has purposefully directed its activities at the forum state by causing a separate person or entity to engage in forum contacts." HealthMarkets, Inc. v. Superior Court (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1160, 1170 (quoting Anglo Irish Bank Corp. v. Superior Court (2009) 165 Cal.App.4th 969, 983). UPC has not done so here. Finally, Plaintiff has not shown evidence of fraudulent conduct by UPC. Plaintiff also fails to show that inequity would result if UPC and UPRC are treated as separate entities. UPC has represented to Plaintiff that UPRC is fully capitalized and capable of satisfying any judgment rendered against it. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant Union Pacific Corporation must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AC

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, September 18, 2018, Line 2, PLAINTIFFS ELISEO SOTO, LIBRADA ZAVALA'S Motion To Compel Depositions, For Production Documents, And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Chuck Geerhart, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted. Defendants shall produce witnesses for deposition and produce documents within 10 days of this order, or on the dates specified in the plaintiffs' amended deposition notices. Plaintiff's request for sanctions denied. Plaintiff failed to comply with notice requirements of CCP sec. 2023.340. Defense sanctions request denied, as it appears there has been a great deal of difficulty in getting these depositions scheduled, making the present motion at least substantially justified. rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, September 18, 2018, Line 11, DEFENDANTs SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT, VALERIE HOSHINO and LISABETH CASTRO-SMYTH's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT The Court denies defendants' motion to strike portions in plaintiff's John Doe's third amended complaint referring to and praying for attorney's fees. Plaintiff has sufficiently alleged facts that could support an award of attorneys' fees under Code of Civ. Proc. § 1021.5 if he prevails. The negligent supervision and failure to report child abuse claims, if proven, affect important public policy concerns. If John Doe prevails, the litigation may confer a significant benefit to students at the school (See Regents of University of California v. Superior Court (2018) 4 Cal.5th 607, 628, ["[e]ven assuming the constitutional provision [Cal. Const., art. I, § 28, subd. (a)(7)] is not self-executing, it clearly expresses a fundamental public policy favoring measures to ensure the safety of California's public school students."](internal citations and quotations omitted).) (See also, Adoption of Joshua S. (2008) 42 Cal.4th 945, 954-955, in cases where fees were awarded "public interest litigation obtained a substantial benefit by causing a change in the defendant's behavior, whose actions or failure to act was somehow impairing the statutory or constitutional rights of the public or a significant class of people."). At this stage, the Court cannot determine whether John Doe can satisfy the financial burden requirement. The analysis would require a comparison of "the expected value of the litigant's own monetary award" and "the actual litigation costs." (Collins v. City of Los Angeles (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 140, 154-155.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ac

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DOE VS. SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT ET AL

Plaintiff avers that he will amend in any event to add other Roe defendants. In the amended complaint, which is due within 30 days of this order, plaintiff must plead with specificity all facts that establish a connection between defendants' actions and plaintiff's alleged damages. This may include allegations regarding how the unavailability of alternative treatment caused plaintiff injury or what that alternative care would have been.
